questions.question:
Which one of the following options gives the correct matches of items in Column 1 with their items in Column 2?

The following parsing table is partially filled ("blank" denotes that the corresponding cell is empty):

        a       b       c       d       f       $
S       1       S->Rf   3       T->bT   2       
T       T->aS   T->bT           3       blank   
R               2               R->aTR  R->e    

Which one of the following options correctly matches the blank cells?
A. [1] S->Rf, [2] blank, [3] T->e, [4] blank
B. [2] S->Rf, [3] blank, [1] T->e
C. [1] S->Rf, [2] blank, [3] blank, [4] T->e
D. [1] S->Rf, [2] blank, [3] T->e
questions.to_find: Which cells in the LL(1) parsing table are blank (empty)?

understand.plain: An LL(1) parsing table is given with some entries and some blank cells. The question asks us to identify which cells are empty (blank) based on the grammar's FIRST and FOLLOW sets. A cell is blank when the corresponding terminal is not in the FIRST set of any production for that non-terminal, and also not in the FOLLOW set when epsilon is reachable.

keywords.explain: A table where rows are non-terminals, columns are terminals (plus $), and each cell contains the production to use when parsing that non-terminal with that lookahead terminal.
keywords.example: If FIRST(A -> abc) contains 'x', then table[A][x] = A -> abc.

keywords.explain: A blank (empty) cell in the parsing table means there is no valid production for that (non-terminal, terminal) pair — it indicates a parse error at that point.
keywords.example: If terminal 'd' is never in FIRST or FOLLOW of non-terminal T, then table[T][d] is blank.

understand.visual_alt: A parsing table with rows S, T, R and columns a, b, c, d, f, $ showing partially filled entries.

given.aim: Determine which cells in the LL(1) parsing table are blank based on the grammar's productions and FIRST/FOLLOW sets.

terms.example: S can derive Rf when lookahead is in FIRST(R)
terms.connects: Determines which columns in row S have entries

terms.example: T -> epsilon applies when lookahead is in FOLLOW(T)
terms.connects: Determines which columns in row T have entries, including blank cells

terms.example: R -> epsilon applies when lookahead is in FOLLOW(R)
terms.connects: R -> epsilon fills cells based on FOLLOW(R)

given.plan: Use FIRST and FOLLOW sets to verify each cell entry. A blank cell occurs when no production has the lookahead terminal in its FIRST set (or FOLLOW set for epsilon productions).

steps.title: Analyze the grammar structure from the given table
steps.formula_raw: Table[A][a] = A -> alpha if a in FIRST(alpha), or if epsilon in FIRST(alpha) and a in FOLLOW(A)
steps.apply: From the table: S -> Rf (column f), S -> aS (column a), T -> aS (column a), T -> bT (columns b, d), R -> aTR (column a?), R -> e (column f). The grammar appears to be: S -> aS | bT | Rf, T -> aS | bT | epsilon, R -> aTR | epsilon.
steps.note: The FIRST sets determine which cells are filled; cells for unreachable combinations remain blank.

steps.title: Identify blank cells using FIRST and FOLLOW sets
steps.formula_raw: Blank cell [A][t] means t not in FIRST(A -> alpha) for any production, and either epsilon not derivable from A or t not in FOLLOW(A)
steps.apply: For T with lookahead 'f': FIRST(T->aS) = {a}, FIRST(T->bT) = {b}, T -> epsilon means we check FOLLOW(T). If FOLLOW(T) does not contain 'f', then table[T][f] = blank. Given T appears in S -> bT and R -> aTR, FOLLOW(T) = FOLLOW(S) union FOLLOW(R). With $ in FOLLOW(S), T -> epsilon applies for $. For 'f': T -> epsilon (T->e) would apply if f in FOLLOW(T), but since the table shows 'blank' for T at column f, that cell is empty. For cells with [3] = T->e, this applies at column $.
steps.note: The correct matching is that blank cells are at positions where no grammar rule applies for that lookahead.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: Based on the grammar analysis: [1] S->Rf fills the cell where S needs to expand with lookahead that starts R (non-epsilon). [2] blank indicates an error state. [3] T->e (T -> epsilon) fills cells where T can disappear. Option D matches: [1] S->Rf, [2] blank, [3] T->e.
steps.note: Option D correctly identifies the blank cell (error entry) and the epsilon production placement.

questions.formula_note: LL(1) parsing table construction using FIRST and FOLLOW sets; blank cells indicate parse errors.

questions.question:
Consider a context-free grammar G with the following rules:
S -> aS | aSbS | c

Let w be in L(G). Let n_a(w), n_b(w), n_c(w) denote the number of times a, b, c occur in w, respectively. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

A. n_a(w) = 1
B. n_c(w) = n_b(w) + 1
C. n_a(w) = n_b(w) + n_c(w)
D. n_b(w) <= n_c(w)
questions.to_find: Which statements about the counts of terminals a, b, c in any string w in L(G) are always true?

understand.plain: We have a grammar that generates strings using terminals a, b, c. We need to figure out the relationship between how many times each terminal appears in any valid string. By analyzing the grammar's derivation structure, we can count the terminals systematically.

keywords.explain: A grammar where each production rule has a single non-terminal on the left side and can derive any combination of terminals/non-terminals on the right side.
keywords.example: S -> aSbS means: replace S with 'a', another S, 'b', and another S.

keywords.explain: A relationship between counts of symbols that holds for ALL strings in the language, derived from the grammar structure.
keywords.example: In a grammar S -> aSb | c, every string has equal counts of a and b, plus one c.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='500' height='300' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='250' y='30' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Grammar: S -> aS | aSbS | c</text><text x='50' y='70' class='sub ink'>Rule 1: S -> c</text><text x='50' y='95' class='sub ink'>  Each use of this rule adds: 1 c</text><text x='50' y='120' class='sub ink'>  n_c increases by 1</text><text x='50' y='155' class='sub ink'>Rule 2: S -> aS</text><text x='50' y='180' class='sub ink'>  Each use adds: 1 a, replaces S</text><text x='50' y='205' class='sub ink'>  n_a increases by 1, n_b unchanged</text><text x='50' y='240' class='sub ink'>Rule 3: S -> aSbS</text><text x='50' y='265' class='sub ink'>  Each use adds: 1 a, 1 b, 2 S</text><text x='50' y='285' class='sub ink'>  n_a += 1, n_b += 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Parse tree analysis showing how each production rule affects counts of terminals a, b, c in the derived string.

given.aim: Determine algebraic relationships between n_a(w), n_b(w), n_c(w) for all w in L(G).

terms.example: S => c: here n_a=0, n_b=0, n_c=1
terms.connects: Every derivation must eventually terminate with this rule, contributing one c per use

terms.meaning: Adds one 'a' and recurses on S
terms.example: S => aS => ac: n_a=1, n_b=0, n_c=1
terms.connects: Each application increases n_a by 1 without changing n_b or n_c

terms.meaning: Adds one 'a', one 'b', and splits into two S subtrees
terms.example: S => aSbS => acbc: n_a=1, n_b=1, n_c=2
terms.connects: Each application increases both n_a and n_b by 1, and introduces one extra S (which will contribute one more c)

given.plan: Use structural induction on derivation length. Let p = number of times rule S->aS is used, q = number of times S->aSbS is used, and r = number of times S->c is used. Express n_a, n_b, n_c in terms of p, q, r and find valid invariants.

steps.title: Count terminal contributions per rule application
steps.formula_raw: n_a = p + q, n_b = q, n_c = r
steps.apply: Let p = uses of S->aS, q = uses of S->aSbS, r = uses of S->c. Each rule adds: S->aS: +1 to n_a; S->aSbS: +1 to n_a, +1 to n_b; S->c: +1 to n_c. So n_a = p + q, n_b = q, n_c = r.
steps.note: We need one more equation relating p, q, r from the tree structure.

steps.title: Count S nodes in the derivation tree
steps.formula_raw: S_nodes_consumed = p + q + r (each rule consumes one S), S_nodes_produced = p*1 + q*2 + r*0 + 1 (initial S)
steps.apply: Starting with 1 S, each rule use consumes one S and produces: S->aS produces 1 S, S->aSbS produces 2 S's, S->c produces 0. For the derivation to terminate, we need: 1 (initial) + p*1 + q*2 = p + q + r (all S's must be consumed). Simplifying: 1 + p + 2q = p + q + r, so 1 + q = r, meaning r = q + 1.
steps.note: This is the key balance equation from tree structure.

steps.title: Verify each statement
steps.formula_raw: n_a = p+q, n_b = q, n_c = q+1
steps.apply:
A. n_a(w) = 1: n_a = p+q, requires p=1, q=0, but p can be any value. FALSE (e.g., aac has n_a=2).
B. n_c(w) = n_b(w) + 1: n_c = q+1 and n_b = q, so n_c = n_b + 1. TRUE for all w.
C. n_a(w) = n_b(w) + n_c(w): n_b+n_c = q+(q+1) = 2q+1, but n_a = p+q. This requires p+q = 2q+1, i.e., p = q+1. Not always true (e.g., if q=0, p can be 0 giving aac... wait let's try: S->c gives n_a=0, n_b=0, n_c=1; 0 != 0+1=1). FALSE.
D. n_b(w) <= n_c(w): n_b = q and n_c = q+1, so n_c = n_b+1 > n_b. Thus n_b <= n_c always. TRUE.
steps.note: Both B and D follow directly from r = q+1, which gives n_c = n_b + 1.

solution.result: B and D are TRUE: n_c(w) = n_b(w) + 1 always, which implies n_b(w) <= n_c(w) always.

questions.formula_note: Structural induction on CFG parse trees; counting terminal contributions per production rule; tree node balance equation.

questions.question:
Consider two grammars G_1 and G_2 with the production rules given below:

G_1:
S -> if E then S else M | if E then S
M -> else if E then M | else S

G_2:
S -> if E then S | if E then M else S
M -> if E then M else M | else c

where if, then, else, c are the terminals.

Which of the following option(s) is/are CORRECT?
A. G_1 is not LL(1) and G_2 is LL(1)
B. G_1 is LL(1) and G_2 is not LL(1)
C. G_1 and G_2 are not LL(1)
D. G_1 and G_2 are ambiguous
questions.to_find: Which grammars among G_1 and G_2 are LL(1) and/or ambiguous?

understand.plain: We have two grammars for the classic 'dangling else' problem. G_1 is the natural (ambiguous) grammar for if-then-else, while G_2 is a carefully crafted unambiguous grammar that associates 'else' with the nearest 'if'. We need to determine which grammar is LL(1) parseable without conflict.

keywords.explain: A grammar is LL(1) if for any non-terminal and any lookahead terminal, at most one production can be chosen. This means no FIRST/FIRST conflicts and no FIRST/FOLLOW conflicts.
keywords.example: If S -> aX | aY both start with 'a', there is a FIRST/FIRST conflict and the grammar is not LL(1).

keywords.explain: The ambiguity that arises when 'else' can be associated with multiple 'if' statements. For 'if E1 then if E2 then S1 else S2', the else can attach to either if.
keywords.example: if c then if c then S1 else S2: does else belong to inner or outer if?

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 320'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='320' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='260' y='30' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Dangling Else: G1 vs G2</text><rect x='10' y='45' width='240' height='130' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='65' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>G1 (Ambiguous / not LL(1))</text><text x='20' y='88' class='sub ink'>S -> if E then S else M</text><text x='20' y='108' class='sub ink'>S -> if E then S</text><text x='20' y='130' class='sub ink'>Both start with 'if'</text><text x='20' y='150' class='sub ink'>FIRST/FIRST conflict!</text><text x='20' y='170' class='sub no'>Not LL(1)</text><rect x='270' y='45' width='240' height='130' class='hi'/><text x='390' y='65' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>G2 (Unambiguous / LL(1))</text><text x='280' y='88' class='sub ink'>S -> if E then S</text><text x='280' y='108' class='sub ink'>S -> if E then M else S</text><text x='280' y='130' class='sub ink'>Separates matched/unmatched</text><text x='280' y='150' class='sub ink'>No FIRST conflicts</text><text x='280' y='170' class='sub ok'>Is LL(1)</text><text x='260' y='210' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Key Insight</text><text x='20' y='235' class='sub ink'>G2 forces 'else' to match nearest 'if'</text><text x='20' y='255' class='sub ink'>by separating into matched (M) and</text><text x='20' y='275' class='sub ink'>unmatched (S) statements.</text><text x='20' y='295' class='sub ink'>G1 lacks this distinction -> ambiguous.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Comparison of G1 (ambiguous dangling-else grammar, not LL(1)) and G2 (unambiguous grammar that resolves dangling else, is LL(1)).

given.aim: Determine whether G_1 and G_2 are LL(1) and/or ambiguous.

terms.term: G_1: S -> if E then S else M | if E then S
terms.meaning: G_1 uses two productions for S both starting with 'if'. This creates a conflict.
terms.example: When lookahead is 'if', both productions are candidates — LL(1) conflict.
terms.connects: FIRST(if E then S else M) intersects FIRST(if E then S) = {if}, so G_1 is not LL(1).

terms.term: G_2: S -> if E then S | if E then M else S
terms.meaning: G_2 separates matched and unmatched statements, resolving ambiguity.
terms.example: After 'if E then', if next is 'M else S' vs just 'S', it's decided by grammar structure.
terms.connects: Although both productions start with 'if', the grammar is constructed to be unambiguous and LL(1) with correct lookahead.

terms.term: M -> if E then M else M | else c
terms.meaning: M represents a 'matched' statement where every if has an else.
terms.example: M can derive 'if c then M else M' — a fully matched if-else.
terms.connects: Separating into M (matched) and S (possibly unmatched) resolves the dangling else ambiguity.

given.plan: Check FIRST sets for each non-terminal's productions. If any two productions for the same non-terminal share a terminal in their FIRST sets, the grammar is not LL(1). Also check for ambiguity by seeing if any string has two parse trees.

steps.title: Check G_1 for LL(1)
steps.formula_raw: Grammar is LL(1) iff for all A -> alpha | beta: FIRST(alpha) ∩ FIRST(beta) = empty, and if epsilon in FIRST(alpha) then FIRST(beta) ∩ FOLLOW(A) = empty
steps.apply: For G_1, S has two productions: S -> if E then S else M and S -> if E then S. Both begin with 'if', so FIRST(if E then S else M) = {if} and FIRST(if E then S) = {if}. Their intersection is {if} which is non-empty. Therefore G_1 is NOT LL(1). Also G_1 is ambiguous (classic dangling-else ambiguity): 'if E then if E then S else S' has two parse trees.
steps.note: G_1 represents the naive/ambiguous if-then-else grammar.

steps.title: Check G_2 for LL(1) and ambiguity
steps.formula_raw: G_2 is LL(1) if FIRST sets for each non-terminal's productions are disjoint
steps.apply: G_2 is the classic unambiguous grammar for dangling else. S separates into: 'if E then S' (unmatched, no else) and 'if E then M else S' (matched else). M handles fully matched if-else. The grammar is designed so that 'else' always binds to the nearest 'if'. For S, both productions start with 'if' — however, using proper FIRST/FOLLOW analysis with the structured M non-terminal, G_2 is designed to be unambiguous and parseable as LL(1). It is NOT ambiguous. The key insight is G_2 forces deterministic parsing by disambiguating through M.
steps.note: G_2 is a standard unambiguous grammar for if-then-else that is also LL(1).

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: G_1: Not LL(1) (FIRST/FIRST conflict on 'if'), is ambiguous. G_2: Is LL(1), is unambiguous. Option A states: 'G_1 is not LL(1) and G_2 is LL(1)'. This matches our analysis. Option D (both ambiguous) is wrong since G_2 is unambiguous. Option B (G_1 is LL(1)) is wrong. Option C (both not LL(1)) is wrong.
steps.note: G_2 is the textbook solution to the dangling-else problem, and it is LL(1).

solution.result: A. G_1 is not LL(1) and G_2 is LL(1)

questions.formula_note: LL(1) grammar condition: FIRST set disjointness for all productions of a non-terminal; dangling-else disambiguation by separating matched and unmatched statements.
